title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
In 2009, the global distribution of excess nitrogen from croplands shows a concentration of high values in specific agricultural regions across Asia and the Americas. The map uses a color scale ranging from light blue for nitrogen mining to dark brown for values reaching 25 million tonnes to visualize the difference between nutrient inputs and crop harvest. The highest values, falling in the darkest brown band representing 10 million to 25 million tonnes, appear in China, India, the United States, and Brazil. These four countries display the most intense coloration on the map, indicating the largest surplus of nitrogen lost to the environment.

Moving to the intermediate bands, countries such as Mexico, Argentina, France, Germany, and Australia appear in shades of orange, corresponding to values between 250,000 tonnes and 1 million tonnes. Canada and Russia also fall within this middle range, showing significant but lower excess nitrogen compared to the top tier. In contrast, large portions of the African continent, including the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Chad, and Niger, along with Greenland and parts of Central Asia, appear in light blue or grey, indicating either nitrogen mining or no available data. The visual pattern places the heaviest nitrogen surplus in the world's major crop-producing nations, while vast areas of the Southern Hemisphere and northern latitudes show minimal excess or data gaps. The distribution highlights a distinct clustering of high nitrogen surplus in the Americas and East and South Asia.
